title: Python Intro
content:
Python Introduction

The Versatile Language

Python is a widely-used, high-level programming language famous for its simple syntax and readability. Because it reads almost like English, it is an excellent first language to learn.



  • Cross-platform (Windows, Mac, Linux).
  • Dominant in Data Analysis, Machine Learning, and AI.
  • Heavily used for web development (Django, Flask) and script automation.

title: Getting Started
content:
Getting Started

Hello World

You write Python code in a text editor or IDE and run it with a Python interpreter. Unlike Java or C++, Python does not require a complex boilerplate or a main() method to print a simple message!

First Program
# Run this code to see the output!
print("Hello, World!")

title: Python Syntax
content:
Python Syntax

Indentation is King

Python syntax emphasizes readability. Most importantly, Python uses indentation (spaces at the beginning of a line) to define code blocks, instead of the curly brackets {} used by JavaScript and Java.

Syntax Rules
# No semicolons required!
if 5 > 2:
    # This line MUST be indented, or Python will throw an error
    print("Five is greater than two!")

title: Comments
content:
Comments

Documenting Code

Comments explain code and are completely ignored when the program is run. Python uses the hash character # for single-line comments.

Comment Syntax
# This is a single-line comment
print("Python is fun!") # You can place comments at the end of a line

'''
This is a
multi-line comment using triple quotes.
It is often used for writing documentation.
'''

title: Variables
content:
Variables

Dynamic Typing

Variables store values. Unlike Java, you do not need to declare a variable's type (like `int` or `String`), nor do you need keywords like `let` or `var`. The variable is created the moment you first assign a value to it.

Variable Creation
x = 5
name = "Sanjith"

# Python is dynamically typed — variable types can change on the fly!
x = "I am now a string!"

print(x)
print(name)

title: Data Types
content:
Data Types

Built-in Types

In Python, variables can store different types of data, and different types can do different things.



  • Text: str (String)
  • Numeric: int (Integer), float (Decimal)
  • Sequence: list, tuple, range
  • Mapping: dict (Dictionary)
  • Set: set
  • Boolean: bool (True/False)

title: Numbers
content:
Numbers

Math in Python

Python can handle integers (whole numbers) and floating-point numbers (decimals) effortlessly.

Math Example
a = 10      # int
b = 3.14    # float

# Print the sum of a and b
print("Total:", a + b)

title: Operators
content:
Operators

Logic and Math

Python operators perform operations on variables and values. Python has beautiful English-based logical operators (and, or, not) rather than symbols (&&, ||, !).

Operator Examples
x = 5
y = 3

print("Addition:", x + y)
print("Comparison:", x == y)
print("Logical:", x > 2 and y < 5)

title: Lists
content:
Lists

Dynamic Arrays

Lists are used to store multiple items in a single variable. In Python, lists are ordered, changeable, and allow duplicate values.

List Syntax
# Lists use square brackets []
mylist = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# Access items by their index (starting at 0)
print("First item:", mylist[0])
print("Full list:", mylist)

title: List Methods
content:
List Methods

Modifying Lists

Python lists come with a set of powerful built-in methods that allow you to easily add, remove, and sort data.

Method Examples
mylist = ["banana", "apple"]

# Add an item to the end
mylist.append("orange")

# Sort alphabetically
mylist.sort()

print(mylist)

title: Tuples
content:
Tuples

Immutable Lists

Tuples are very similar to Lists, but they are unchangeable (immutable). Once a tuple is created, you cannot add, remove, or change its items. They are excellent for storing data that should never be altered.

Tuple Syntax
# Tuples use parentheses ()
thistuple = ("apple", "banana", "cherry")

print(thistuple)
# thistuple.append("orange") # Un-commenting this will cause an error!

title: Dictionaries
content:
Dictionaries

Key-Value Pairs

Dictionaries store data in key: value pairs (similar to JSON objects). They are ordered (as of Python 3.7), changeable, and do not allow duplicate keys.



Dictionary Example
thisdict = {
  "brand": "Ford",
  "model": "Mustang",
  "year": 1964
}

# Access values using their key
print("Brand:", thisdict["brand"])

# Add a new key-value pair
thisdict["color"] = "Red"
print(thisdict)

title: If...Else
content:
If...Else

Control Flow

Conditional statements control the flow of your code. Python uses if, elif (else if), and else.

Conditional Logic
x = 10
y = 20

if x > y:
    print("x is greater")
elif x == y:
    print("x and y are equal")
else:
    print("y is greater")

title: While Loops
content:
While Loops

Condition-Based Loops

A while loop executes a set of statements as long as a condition remains true.

While Loop Example
i = 0
while i < 5:
    print("Count:", i)
    i += 1 # Remember to increment i, or the loop will run forever!

title: For Loops
content:
For Loops

Iterating Sequences

A for loop is used for iterating over a sequence (that is either a list, a tuple, a dictionary, a set, or a string). It is much simpler than a For loop in C++ or Java.

For Loop Example
mylist = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# Read it like English: "For each item in mylist..."
for item in mylist:
    print(item)
    
# You can also loop through a range of numbers
for x in range(3):
    print("Range loop:", x)

title: Functions
content:
Functions

Reusable Code Blocks

Functions are defined using the def keyword. They allow you to write a block of code once and run it many times, passing in different arguments each time.

Def Keyword
def greet(name):
    print("Hello, " + name + "!")

# Call the function
greet("Sanjith")
greet("Python Developer")

title: File Handling
content:
File Handling

Reading and Writing

Python has incredibly easy built-in functions for creating, reading, updating, and deleting files. The key function is open().

Working with Files
# 'w' means Write mode (creates the file if it doesn't exist)
with open("file.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello File! Python makes this easy.")

# 'r' means Read mode
with open("file.txt", "r") as f:
    print(f.read())

title: Classes & Objects
content:
Classes and Objects

Object-Oriented Python

Almost everything in Python is an object. A Class is like an object constructor, or a "blueprint" for creating objects.



Class Creation
# Define the blueprint
class MyClass:
    x = 5

# Build a specific object from the blueprint
p1 = MyClass()

print("The property x inside p1 is:", p1.x)

title: Modules
content:
Modules

Code Libraries

A module is a file containing a set of functions you want to include in your application. Python comes with a massive standard library, and you can import tools using the import keyword.

Import Statement
# Import the built-in Math module
import math

# Use methods attached to the module
result = math.sqrt(25)
print("The square root of 25 is:", result)

title: Basics Summary
content:
Python Basics Summary

A Strong Foundation

You have successfully learned the fundamentals of Python! Its clean, readable syntax makes powerful operations incredibly easy to write.

  • Syntax & Types: Dynamic variables, indentation, Strings, and Numbers.
  • Data Structures: Lists, Tuples, and Dictionaries for organizing data.
  • Logic & Flow: For/While loops, If/Else statements, and custom Functions.

Ready for more? Let's dive into advanced Data Science tools and modern backend frameworks next!

title: Sets
content:
Python Sets

Unique Collections

Sets are unordered collections of unique elements. If you try to add a duplicate item to a set, Python will simply ignore it. They are incredibly useful for removing duplicates from lists or performing mathematical operations like unions and intersections.


[Image of Python sets intersection union Venn diagram]
Set Syntax
# Sets use curly braces {}
myset = {"apple", "banana", "cherry"}

# Adding a duplicate does nothing!
myset.add("apple")
myset.add("orange")

print(myset)

title: String Methods
content:
String Methods

Manipulating Text

Python strings are objects, meaning they come with a variety of built-in methods to format, split, and search through text easily.

  • upper() — Converts the string to uppercase.
  • lower() — Converts the string to lowercase.
  • split() — Splits a string into a list based on a separator.
  • replace() — Replaces specific substrings.
Using Methods
txt = "Hello World"

print("Uppercase:", txt.upper())
print("Replaced:", txt.replace("World", "Python"))
print("Split:", txt.split(" ")) # Splits by space into a List

title: List Comprehension
content:
List Comprehension

Pythonic Lists

List comprehension is a beloved Python feature. It offers a shorter, more concise syntax to create a new list based on the values of an existing list, combining a for loop and an if statement into a single line.



Comprehension Example
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "kiwi", "mango"]

# Create a new list containing only fruits with the letter "a" in them
newlist = [x for x in fruits if "a" in x]

print(newlist)

title: Iterators
content:
Iterators

Traversing Data

An iterator is an object that contains a countable number of values. It allows you to traverse through all the values, one by one, using the next() and iter() methods without needing a traditional loop or index numbers.

The iter() Method
mytuple = ("apple", "banana", "cherry")

# Create an iterator from the tuple
myit = iter(mytuple)

# Call next() to step through the data one by one
print(next(myit))
print(next(myit))
print(next(myit))

title: Date & Time
content:
Date & Time

The Datetime Module

Python does not have a built-in date data type, but we can import the built-in datetime module to work with dates as date objects.

Working with Dates
import datetime

# Get the current date and time
now = datetime.datetime.now()

print("Full Date/Time:", now)
print("Current Year:", now.year)

# Format the date into a readable string (e.g., %B = Full Month Name)
print("Formatted:", now.strftime("%B %d, %Y"))

title: JSON in Python
content:
Python JSON

Data Interchange

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight format for storing and transporting data, heavily used in APIs. Python has a built-in package called json to parse JSON text into Python Dictionaries, and vice versa.

Parsing JSON
import json

# A Python Dictionary
x = {"name": "John", "age": 30, "city": "New York"}

# json.dumps() converts a Python object into a JSON string
j = json.dumps(x)
print("JSON String:", j)

# json.loads() converts a JSON string back into a Python Dictionary
parsed = json.loads(j)
print("Back to Dictionary:", parsed["name"])

title: Try...Except
content:
Try…Except

Handling Errors

When an error occurs, Python will normally stop and generate an error message. Use try to test a block of code for errors, and except to handle the error gracefully without crashing your app.

Error Catching
try:
    # 'undefined_var' does not exist, so this will throw an error!
    print(undefined_var)
except NameError:
    print("Caught an error: Variable is not defined!")
except:
    print("Something else went wrong.")
finally:
    print("The 'finally' block always executes, regardless of errors.")

title: User Input
content:
User Input

Interactive Scripts

You can prompt the user to enter data into the console using the input() function. The program will pause execution and wait for the user to type something and press Enter.

Input Function
# Prompt the user
name = input("Enter your name: ")

# All input is captured as a String!
print("Hello, " + name + "! Welcome to Python.")

title: Virtual Environments
content:
Virtual Environments

Isolating Dependencies

When building different Python projects, you might need different versions of the same library. A virtual environment (venv) creates an isolated space for a project so its dependencies don't conflict with other projects or your main operating system.



Terminal Commands
# Run these in your terminal, not in a Python file!

# 1. Create a virtual environment named 'myenv'
python -m venv myenv

# 2. Activate it on Mac/Linux:
source myenv/bin/activate

# OR Activate it on Windows:
myenv\Scripts\activate

title: Intermediate Summary
content:
Python Summary — Intermediate

Leveling Up

You have successfully completed the intermediate concepts of Python!

  • Data Structures: Managing unique items with Sets and mastering String/List methods.
  • Pythonic Code: Writing clean, one-line loops using List Comprehensions.
  • System Operations: Parsing JSON data, working with Date/Time, and handling Errors.
  • Project Management: Securing your app's dependencies using Virtual Environments.

These tools make your Python programming significantly more powerful. Let's move on to the advanced concepts!

title: Built-in Functions
content:
Built-in Functions

Ready-to-Use Tools

Python comes with many built-in functions that are always available. They help perform common tasks quickly without requiring you to write custom loops or logic from scratch.

Common Built-ins
my_list = [10, 20, 30]
my_string = "Python"

# len() gets the length of an object
print("List length:", len(my_list))

# type() tells you the data type
print("String type:", type(my_string))

# max() and min() find the highest/lowest values
print("Highest number:", max(my_list))

title: Modules & Packages
content:
Modules and Packages

Organizing Code

A module is simply a Python file (with a .py extension) containing reusable code—like functions and classes. A package is a folder that contains multiple modules. You can bring modules into your current file using the import keyword.



Importing Modules
# Import an entire module
import math
print(math.sqrt(25))

# Import a specific function FROM a module
from math import pi
print("Pi is roughly:", pi)

# Import and give it an 'alias' (a nickname)
import datetime as dt
print(dt.datetime.now())

title: Standard Library
content:
Standard Library Modules

Batteries Included

Python is famous for having "batteries included." This refers to the Python Standard Library—a massive collection of built-in modules you get immediately upon installing Python, without needing to download anything extra.

  • math: Advanced mathematical operations.
  • random: Generating random data.
  • os / sys: Interacting with the computer's operating system.
  • json / csv: Reading and writing specific data formats.

title: OS Module
content:
The os Module

Operating System Access

The os module allows your Python script to interact directly with the operating system. You can navigate folders, create new directories, check if files exist, and delete files.

Folder Navigation
import os

# Get Current Working Directory (Where is this script running?)
cwd = os.getcwd()
print("Running in:", cwd)

# List all files and folders in the current directory
contents = os.listdir(cwd)
print("Folder contents:", contents)

# Create a new folder (Uncomment to test if running locally)
# os.mkdir('my_new_folder')

title: Math Module
content:
The math Module

Advanced Calculations

While basic arithmetic (+, -, *, /) is built into core Python, the math module provides access to deeper mathematical functions and constants defined by the C standard.

Math Operations
import math

print("Value of Pi:", math.pi)

# Rounding numbers up or down
print("Ceiling of 4.2:", math.ceil(4.2))   # Rounds UP to 5
print("Floor of 4.8:", math.floor(4.8))     # Rounds DOWN to 4

# Power and Square Root
print("3 to the power of 2:", math.pow(3, 2))
print("Square root of 16:", math.sqrt(16))

title: Random Module
content:
The random Module

Generating Uncertainty

The random module is used to generate pseudo-random numbers, make random choices from lists, or shuffle data. It is widely used in games, testing, and data sampling.

Randomizing Data
import random

# Pick a random integer between 1 and 10
print("Random number:", random.randint(1, 10))

# Pick a random choice from a list
options = ["Rock", "Paper", "Scissors"]
print("Computer chose:", random.choice(options))

# Shuffle a list in place
cards = ["Ace", "King", "Queen", "Jack"]
random.shuffle(cards)
print("Shuffled hand:", cards)

title: PIP & Packages
content:
PIP & External Packages

The Python Ecosystem

What if the tool you need isn't in the Standard Library? PIP (Pip Installs Packages) is Python's package manager. It allows you to instantly download and install hundreds of thousands of third-party libraries from the Python Package Index (PyPI).



Terminal Commands
# NOTE: These commands are run in your Terminal/Command Prompt, NOT in a Python file!

# Install a third-party package (like 'requests' for APIs)
pip install requests

# View all packages currently installed on your system
pip list

# Remove a package you no longer need
pip uninstall requests

title: Requests Module
content:
The requests Module

Communicating with the Web

The requests module is the absolute industry standard for making HTTP requests in Python. Though it must be installed via PIP, it is so widely used that it feels like a built-in module. It is how Python talks to REST APIs.



Fetching API Data
import requests

# Note: This code requires 'requests' to be installed locally!
try:
    # Send a GET request to a public API
    response = requests.get('https://api.github.com')
    
    # Check the HTTP Status Code (200 means OK!)
    print("Status Code:", response.status_code)
    
    # If the response is JSON, parse it automatically into a dictionary!
    data = response.json()
    print("Current User URL endpoint:", data["current_user_url"])
except Exception as e:
    print("Request failed. Ensure the requests module is installed.")

title: Debugging
content:
Python Debugging Tips

Finding and Fixing Bugs

Every developer writes bugs. Debugging is the process of hunting them down. Here are the most effective ways to debug Python code:



  • The Print Method: Temporarily place print(variable_name) throughout your code to trace how data changes line by line.
  • Check Types: Python is dynamically typed. A common bug is trying to do math on a string. Always use print(type(x)) if your data looks wrong.
  • IDE Debuggers: Modern editors like VS Code and PyCharm have built-in debuggers. You can set "Breakpoints" (red dots) to pause code execution entirely and inspect every variable in real-time.

title: Advanced Summary
content:
Python Complete Course Summary 🚀

You Did It! 🏆

Congratulations! You have completed the comprehensive Python curriculum. You have evolved from writing simple print statements to utilizing the massive ecosystem of Python packages.

  • Fundamentals: Dynamic Variables, Data Types, If/Else Logic, and Loops.
  • Data Mastery: Lists, Tuples, Dictionaries, Sets, and List Comprehensions.
  • Architecture: Classes, Objects, Modules, and Virtual Environments.
  • The World Outside: File I/O, Error Handling, PIP, and Web Requests.

Python is an incredibly powerful tool. Your next step is to pick a specialization: will you build web backends with Django/Flask, or dive into Data Science with Pandas and NumPy?
